questions.fact: Anthony Babington was just 25 years old when he plotted to kill the Queen, and had actually served as a secret page boy for Mary, Queen of Scots when he was a teenager.
distractors: Francis Throckmorton. He was a young, wealthy Catholic who foolishly detailed a six-step plan to assassinate Queen Elizabeth.
Roberto Ridolfi. He was a young, wealthy Catholic who foolishly detailed a six-step plan to assassinate Queen Elizabeth.
Thomas Howard. He was a young, wealthy Catholic who foolishly detailed a six-step plan to assassinate Queen Elizabeth.

questions.question: How did Babington attempt to communicate secretly with Mary?
questions.answer: By using complex ciphers (secret codes). He hid these coded letters inside beer barrels being delivered to her captivity.
questions.fact: The brewer delivering the beer was secretly working for Walsingham, meaning Walsingham's spies read every single coded letter before it ever reached Mary!
distractors: By hiding messages inside hollow walking sticks. He hid these coded letters inside beer barrels being delivered to her captivity.
By writing messages in invisible ink on the back of prayer books. He hid these coded letters inside beer barrels being delivered to her captivity.
By bribing Mary's guards to carry oral messages. He hid these coded letters inside beer barrels being delivered to her captivity.

questions.question: What vital role did Sir Francis Walsingham hold in Elizabeth's government?
questions.answer: He was her Secretary of State and 'Spy Master'. He built a vast, ruthless intelligence network across Europe to protect the Queen.
questions.fact: Walsingham funded much of his European spy network out of his own pocket, dying heavily in debt because he spent so much personal wealth protecting the Queen.
distractors: He was her Lord Treasurer and chief financial advisor. He built a vast, ruthless intelligence network across Europe to protect the Queen.
He was her Archbishop of Canterbury and religious advisor. He built a vast, ruthless intelligence network across Europe to protect the Queen.
He was her Lord Chancellor and chief judicial officer. He built a vast, ruthless intelligence network across Europe to protect the Queen.

questions.question: What is an 'agent provocateur'?
questions.answer: A spy who actively encourages others to commit a crime to trap them. Walsingham used these agents to push Catholic plotters into putting their treason in writing.
questions.fact: Gilbert Gifford was the famous double-agent provocateur who infiltrated the Babington plot, pretending to be a loyal Catholic while secretly passing all letters directly to Walsingham.
distractors: A double agent who feeds false information to foreign governments. Walsingham used these agents to mislead Spanish ambassadors.
A code-breaker who deciphers secret messages intercepted by spies. Walsingham used these agents to find evidence of treason.
A royal guard who protects the Queen from assassination attempts. Walsingham used these agents to secure the palaces.

questions.question: Who was the expert code-breaker employed by Walsingham to decipher the Babington letters?
questions.answer: Thomas Phelippes. His ability to break the secret ciphers provided the undeniable written proof that Mary supported Elizabeth's assassination.
questions.fact: Phelippes didn't just decode Mary's letter; he actually forged a postscript (an addition) to the end of it, asking Babington to provide the names of the six assassins so Walsingham could arrest them!
distractors: Robert Poley. His ability to break the secret ciphers provided the undeniable written proof that Mary supported Elizabeth's assassination.
Gilbert Gifford. His ability to break the secret ciphers provided the undeniable written proof that Mary supported Elizabeth's assassination.
Arthur Gregory. His ability to break the secret ciphers provided the undeniable written proof that Mary supported Elizabeth's assassination.

questions.question: In what month and year was Mary, Queen of Scots executed?
questions.answer: February 1587. She was beheaded at Fotheringhay Castle after 19 years of captivity in England.
questions.fact: The executioner bungled the beheading; it took three swings of the axe to sever her head, and her pet dog was famously found hiding under her dress.
distractors: October 1586. She was beheaded at Fotheringhay Castle after 19 years of captivity in England.
August 1586. She was beheaded at Fotheringhay Castle after 19 years of captivity in England.
March 1587. She was beheaded at Fotheringhay Castle after 19 years of captivity in England.

questions.question: Which specific plot provided the final evidence that led to Mary's execution?
questions.answer: The Babington Plot (1586). Walsingham's interception of the letters proved she was actively involved in treason.
questions.fact: At her trial, Mary powerfully argued that because she was a Scottish monarch and not an English citizen, she could not legally be found guilty of English treason.
distractors: The Ridolfi Plot (1571). Walsingham's interception of the letters proved she was actively involved in treason.
The Throckmorton Plot (1583). Walsingham's interception of the letters proved she was actively involved in treason.
The Norfolk Plot (1569). Walsingham's interception of the letters proved she was actively involved in treason.

questions.question: What did Mary explicitly agree to in her coded reply to Babington?
questions.answer: She agreed to the assassination of Queen Elizabeth. This undeniable written consent left Elizabeth with no political excuse to keep her alive.
questions.fact: Mary's defenders have historically argued that Walsingham's spies may have entirely forged her reply, though most modern historians believe the letter was absolutely genuine.
distractors: The invasion of England by a French army. This undeniable written consent left Elizabeth with no political excuse to keep her alive.
The marriage to the Duke of Norfolk to claim the crown. This undeniable written consent left Elizabeth with no political excuse to keep her alive.
The transfer of the English succession to King Philip II. This undeniable written consent left Elizabeth with no political excuse to keep her alive.

questions.question: Which 1585 law stated that Mary would be executed if she was found guilty of plotting?
questions.answer: The Act for the Preservation of the Queen's Safety. This law laid the legal groundwork to bypass Elizabeth's reluctance and force the execution.
questions.fact: This Act was pushed through Parliament heavily by William Cecil and Walsingham, specifically because they were terrified Elizabeth would be assassinated like the Dutch Protestant leader William of Orange.
distractors: The Act Against Recusancy. This law laid the legal groundwork to bypass Elizabeth's reluctance and force the execution.
The Act of Supremacy. This law laid the legal groundwork to bypass Elizabeth's reluctance and force the execution.
The Act of Uniformity. This law laid the legal groundwork to bypass Elizabeth's reluctance and force the execution.

questions.question: Why was Elizabeth so hesitant to sign Mary's death warrant?
questions.answer: Because Mary was an 'anointed monarch'. Elizabeth feared that executing a fellow queen chosen by God would justify her own assassination.
questions.fact: After signing the warrant, Elizabeth claimed she only meant it to be kept as a threat, and she threw her secretary, William Davison, into the Tower of London for actually delivering it!
distractors: Because she feared a massive rebellion among English Protestants who opposed executions. She feared this would destabilize the South.
Because she believed Mary was innocent and was being framed by Walsingham. She feared this would destroy her own reputation.
Because she had promised the Scottish King, James VI, that she would never hurt his mother. She feared this would lead to a Scottish war.

questions.question: What happened to the domestic Catholic threat in England after Mary's execution?
questions.answer: It severely declined. With Mary dead, English Catholics permanently lost their legitimate alternative to the throne, leaving plotters without a figurehead.
questions.fact: Mary's death left English Catholics utterly demoralised; they had waited 19 years for her to take the throne, and her death destroyed any realistic hope of a domestic Catholic uprising.
distractors: It increased significantly. With Mary dead, English Catholics were outraged and launched a series of violent regional rebellions.
It remained completely unchanged. English Catholics continued to plot with Spanish ambassadors to assassinate the Queen.
It resulted in the Pope issuing a new excommunication order. This convinced English Catholics to boycott all Protestant services.

questions.question: How did King Philip II of Spain react to Mary's execution?
questions.answer: It gave him the final religious justification to launch the Armada. He now viewed invading England as a holy crusade to avenge a martyred Catholic queen.
questions.fact: Before her death, Mary had secretly willed her claim to the English throne directly to King Philip II of Spain, giving him a legal dynastic excuse to invade England.
distractors: He signed a peace treaty with England to avoid further conflict. He now realized that England was too strong to defeat.
He demanded that Elizabeth pay compensation to Mary's family in Scotland. He threatened to launch an embargo if she refused.
He offered to marry Elizabeth to unite the two crowns and end the conflict. He now saw her as a legitimate and strong ruler.

questions.question: What relation was Mary to the new Protestant King of Scotland, James VI?
questions.answer: She was his mother. However, James VI did not protest her execution because Elizabeth secretly promised him the English throne.
questions.fact: James VI lodged a formal diplomatic protest to save face, but he accepted an annual pension of £4,000 from Elizabeth to essentially look the other way.
distractors: She was his sister. However, James VI did not protest her execution because Elizabeth secretly promised him the English throne.
She was his aunt. However, James VI did not protest her execution because Elizabeth secretly promised him the English throne.
She was his cousin. However, James VI did not protest her execution because Elizabeth secretly promised him the English throne.

questions.question: How did Mary's execution change the future of the English succession?
questions.answer: It removed the main Catholic heir. This guaranteed that the next monarch would be a Protestant, securing the Religious Settlement.
questions.fact: Ironically, by executing the Catholic Mary, Elizabeth ensured that Mary's Protestant son, James VI of Scotland, would peacefully unite the crowns of England and Scotland in 1603.
distractors: It forced Elizabeth to name King Philip II of Spain as her legal heir. This guaranteed that the next monarch would be a Catholic.
It forced Elizabeth to marry Robert Dudley to try and produce a Protestant heir. This guaranteed that the next monarch would be Tudor.
It allowed Parliament to select the next monarch from the English nobility. This guaranteed that the next monarch would be Protestant.

questions.question: What did the execution prove about the power of Elizabeth's Privy Council?
questions.answer: It proved they could legally force the execution of a foreign queen to protect the state. The Council even delivered the death warrant behind Elizabeth's back.
questions.fact: The Privy Council secretly met without Elizabeth’s knowledge to dispatch the death warrant to Fotheringhay Castle, knowing the Queen might change her mind again if they waited.
distractors: It proved they could dissolve Parliament and rule England as a military council to protect the state.
It proved they could arrest and put the Queen on trial if she refused to sign death warrants to protect the state.
It proved they could make treaties with foreign Protestant powers without royal approval to protect the state.

questions.question: What religion was King Philip II of Spain?
questions.answer: He was a devout, strict Catholic. He believed it was his holy duty from God to stamp out Protestantism across Europe.
questions.fact: Philip II had actually been the King Consort of England when he was married to Elizabeth's sister, Mary I, and he had personally helped her burn nearly 300 English Protestants.
distractors: He was a moderate Protestant. He believed it was his duty to support the new Church of England against radical Puritans.
He was a strict Calvinist. He believed it was his duty to spread the Reformation across Europe and defeat the Catholic Pope.
He was a traditional humanist. He believed it was his duty to promote education and classic studies throughout his empire.

questions.question: How did the 1570 Papal Bull increase political tension between England and Spain?
questions.answer: It gave Spain religious permission to actively plot against Elizabeth. Because the Pope declared her a heretic, Philip II felt justified in helping Catholic assassins.
questions.fact: English Protestant propaganda began portraying Spain not just as a political rival, but as the literal 'Antichrist', fuelling intense public paranoia against any Spanish presence in London.
distractors: It banned all Spanish merchants from trading with English ports. Because the Pope excommunicated Elizabeth, Philip II halted the wool trade.
It ordered Spain to pay a special tax to Rome to fund the war in the Netherlands. Because of this, Philip II could not afford to build the Armada.
It allowed English privateers to legally raid Spanish colonies without a licence. Because of this, Philip II felt justified in attacking Drake.

questions.question: Which terrifying Catholic alliance did Spain sign with France in 1584?
questions.answer: The Treaty of Joinville. This united the two most powerful Catholic superpowers in Europe with the specific goal of eradicating Protestantism.
questions.fact: This treaty terrified Elizabeth because it meant England could no longer play France and Spain off against each other; the two Catholic giants were now united against her.
distractors: The Treaty of Nonsuch. This united the two most powerful Catholic superpowers in Europe with the specific goal of eradicating Protestantism.
The Treaty of Sèvres. This united the two most powerful Catholic superpowers in Europe with the specific goal of eradicating Protestantism.
The Protocol of Joinville. This united the two most powerful Catholic superpowers in Europe with the specific goal of eradicating Protestantism.

questions.question: What region of the world did Spain hold a strict trade monopoly over?
questions.answer: The New World (The Americas). Spain claimed that all trade in this region required a Spanish licence, shutting out English merchants.
questions.fact: Spain claimed this total monopoly based on the 1494 Treaty of Tordesillas, a decree by the Pope that literally divided the newly discovered Americas between Spain and Portugal.
distractors: The East Indies (Southeast Asia). Spain claimed that all trade in this region required a Spanish licence, shutting out English merchants.
The Baltic Sea (Northern Europe). Spain claimed that all trade in this region required a Spanish licence, shutting out English merchants.
The Mediterranean (Southern Europe). Spain claimed that all trade in this region required a Spanish licence, shutting out English merchants.

questions.question: What highly profitable goods were grown and mined in the Spanish New World?
questions.answer: Tobacco, sugar, and vast amounts of silver. This wealth funded Spain's massive armies in Europe.
questions.fact: The Spanish silver mines at Potosí (in modern-day Bolivia) produced so much wealth that they single-handedly caused massive inflation across the entire European continent.
distractors: Wheat, barley, and vast amounts of gold. This wealth funded Spain's massive armies in Europe.
Spices, silk, and vast amounts of copper. This wealth funded Spain's massive armies in Europe.
Cotton, tea, and vast amounts of iron. This wealth funded Spain's massive armies in Europe.

questions.question: Why did English merchants start trading illegally in the New World?
questions.answer: Because Spain had disrupted their traditional cloth trade in Antwerp. English merchants desperately needed new markets to survive economically.
questions.fact: English merchants found they could sell goods in the Spanish colonies for massive profits because the Spanish settlers there were desperate for basic European supplies like cloth and iron.
distractors: Because the French had blockaded their traditional trade routes in the Mediterranean. English merchants desperately needed new markets.
Because the Pope had excommunicated Elizabeth, banning them from trading in Italy. English merchants desperately needed new markets.
Because the gentry had raised land rents, making farming unprofitable. English merchants desperately needed new businesses.

questions.question: How did Spain react to John Hawkins trading enslaved people in the New World in 1568?
questions.answer: They aggressively attacked his fleet at San Juan de Ulúa. This battle destroyed most of his ships and sparked a deep hatred of Spain among English sailors.
questions.fact: Hawkins lost 4 of his 6 ships in the Spanish ambush, and one of the surviving captains was his young cousin, Francis Drake, who swore a lifelong vendetta against Spain.
distractors: They welcomed him to trade and granted him a formal Spanish license. This trade agreement brought temporary peace between the two nations.
They ignored his actions because they needed the labor for their plantations. This allowed Hawkins to make a massive profit for his investors.
They petitioned Queen Elizabeth to arrest Hawkins and seize his gold. This diplomatic request was refused, creating tension between the countries.

questions.question: What was the 'Genoese Loan' that Elizabeth seized in 1568?
questions.answer: Gold lent by Italian bankers to pay the Spanish army. Elizabeth seized it while the ships sheltered in English ports, causing a massive diplomatic crisis.
questions.fact: In direct retaliation for the Genoese Loan, Spain embargoed (banned) all English trade to the Netherlands, plunging the English cloth industry into a severe economic depression.
distractors: Gold lent by the Pope to fund Catholic plots. Elizabeth seized it while the ships sheltered in English ports, causing a massive diplomatic crisis.
Gold lent by Italian merchants to fund English privateers. Elizabeth seized it while the ships sheltered in English ports, causing a crisis.
Gold lent by Spanish bankers to fund the Dutch rebels. Elizabeth seized it while the ships sheltered in English ports, causing a crisis.

questions.question: How did Spain's actions in the Netherlands increase religious rivalry?
questions.answer: The brutal Spanish Inquisition against Dutch Protestants horrified the English. Elizabethans feared the Spanish army would cross the Channel and do the same to them.
questions.fact: Many English Protestants volunteered to go and fight in the Netherlands as independent mercenaries long before Elizabeth officially sent the royal army in 1585.
distractors: The Spanish Inquisition's decision to support Puritan priests in the Netherlands horrified the English. They feared the same would happen to them.
The Spanish army's decision to destroy Catholic churches in the Netherlands horrified the English. They feared the same would happen to them.
The Spanish Inquisition's decision to ban all trade with Protestant merchants in the Netherlands horrified the English. They feared the same.

questions.question: Why did Elizabeth secretly fund English pirates instead of officially declaring war?
questions.answer: To weaken Spain's economy and enrich England while avoiding the massive costs of a formal war.
questions.fact: By secretly funding privateers, Elizabeth maintained 'plausible deniability' - she could steal Spanish gold but officially tell Philip II that the pirates were acting entirely without her permission.
distractors: To build a joint naval coalition with France and the Netherlands. By funding pirates, she prepared for a coordinated war against Spain.
To force Spain to pay compensation for the lost strategic port of Calais. By plundering gold, she recovered the value of the port.
To satisfy the demands of the merchant class who wanted to colonize the Americas. By funding pirates, she explored potential settlement sites.

questions.question: What was a 'privateer'?
questions.answer: A sailor officially licensed and funded by the government to attack enemy ships. They were essentially legal pirates acting on behalf of the Queen.
questions.fact: The official royal document giving a privateer legal permission to attack enemy ships was called a 'Letter of Marque'. Without it, they would be hanged as common pirates.
distractors: A royal guard stationed at the naval ports to inspect incoming merchant cargo. They were essentially customs officers acting on behalf of the Queen.
A French mercenary hired to defend English shipping lines from Spanish raids. They were essentially paid soldiers acting on behalf of the Queen.
An explorer given a royal charter to establish a new colony in the New World. They were essentially royal governors acting on behalf of the Queen.

questions.question: Who was England's most famous and successful privateer?
questions.answer: Sir Francis Drake. He was a brilliant sailor who sought revenge against the Spanish for the attack at San Juan de Ulúa.
questions.fact: The Spanish terrified their children with tales of Drake, nicknaming him 'El Draque' (The Dragon), and King Philip II placed a massive bounty on his head.
distractors: Sir Walter Raleigh. He was a famous courtier and explorer who drove the Virginia project forward.
Sir John Hawkins. He established the triangular trade route and designed new galleons.
Sir Richard Grenville. He was a naval commander who led the first group of colonists to Roanoke.

questions.question: In what years did Drake successfully circumnavigate the globe?
questions.answer: 1577–1580. He became the first Englishman to sail completely around the world, surviving the incredibly dangerous voyage.
questions.fact: Drake's crew became only the second expedition in world history to circumnavigate the globe, following the famous fleet of Ferdinand Magellan.
distractors: 1572–1575. He became the first Englishman to sail completely around the world, surviving the incredibly dangerous voyage.
1585–1588. He became the first Englishman to sail completely around the world, surviving the incredibly dangerous voyage.
1590–1593. He became the first Englishman to sail completely around the world, surviving the incredibly dangerous voyage.

questions.question: What was the name of Drake's surviving ship during his circumnavigation?
questions.answer: The Golden Hind. He used it to constantly raid unprotected Spanish colonial ports along the coast of South America.
questions.fact: The ship was originally named The Pelican, but Drake renamed it mid-voyage to The Golden Hind to flatter one of his wealthy aristocratic investors, Sir Christopher Hatton.
distractors: The Tiger. He used it to constantly raid unprotected Spanish colonial ports along the coast of South America.
The Falcon. He used it to constantly raid unprotected Spanish colonial ports along the coast of South America.
The Revenge. He used it to constantly raid unprotected Spanish colonial ports along the coast of South America.

questions.question: What region in North America did Drake claim for Elizabeth in 1579?
questions.answer: Nova Albion (modern-day California). He crowned himself in the name of the Queen to establish an English presence in the Americas.
questions.fact: Drake left a brass plate nailed to a post in California claiming the land for Queen Elizabeth, though the exact location of his landing remains a historical mystery today.
distractors: Virginia (modern-day North Carolina). He crowned himself in the name of the Queen to establish an English presence in the Americas.
New England (modern-day Massachusetts). He crowned himself in the name of the Queen to establish an English presence in the Americas.
Florida (modern-day Miami). He crowned himself in the name of the Queen to establish an English presence in the Americas.

questions.question: How did claiming Nova Albion challenge Spain and the Pope?
questions.answer: It directly defied the Pope's decree that the Americas belonged exclusively to Spain. It signaled that England intended to build its own global empire.
questions.fact: Drake's presence in the Pacific Ocean shocked the Spanish, as they had previously believed the Pacific was a 'Spanish Lake' completely safe from any English ships.
distractors: It resulted in Spain declaring an immediate trade embargo on all English cloth exports. It signaled that Spain would defend its trade route.
It forced the French King to sign the Treaty of Joinville with Scotland. It signaled that France would protect its trade in Canada.
It allowed Elizabeth to appoint Drake as the royal governor of the Americas. It signaled that England would build a land base in Florida.

questions.question: Roughly how much stolen Spanish treasure did Drake return with in 1580?
questions.answer: £400,000. This was an astonishing amount of money that was immediately used to pay off England's national debt.
questions.fact: Drake captured a massive Spanish treasure galleon called the Cacafuego; it was so heavily laden with silver and jewels that it took the English crew six days to unload it!
distractors: £50,000. This was an astonishing amount of money that was immediately used to pay off England's national debt.
£100,000. This was an astonishing amount of money that was immediately used to pay off England's national debt.
£1,000,000. This was an astonishing amount of money that was immediately used to pay off England's national debt.

questions.question: What percentage profit did Drake's private investors make from his circumnavigation?
questions.answer: Around 4700%. This incredible profit spurred a massive boom in English global exploration and private investment.
questions.fact: Queen Elizabeth’s personal share of the stolen treasure was over £160,000, which was more than enough to cover the entire cost of running the English government for a full year.
distractors: Around 100%. This incredible profit spurred a massive boom in English global exploration and private investment.
Around 500%. This incredible profit spurred a massive boom in English global exploration and private investment.
Around 1000%. This incredible profit spurred a massive boom in English global exploration and private investment.

questions.question: How did Queen Elizabeth reward Drake for his actions against the Spanish?
questions.answer: She publicly knighted him on the deck of the Golden Hind. This elevated him from a common sailor to a revered national hero.
questions.fact: Elizabeth cleverly asked the French ambassador to perform the actual sword-dubbing ceremony on the ship, forcing Catholic France to publicly support Drake's actions against Spain.
distractors: She appointed him as the Lord High Admiral of the Navy. This elevated him from a common sailor to a revered national hero.
She granted him vast estates in the North of England. This elevated him from a common sailor to a revered national hero.
She named him as her chief Secretary of State. This elevated him from a common sailor to a revered national hero.

questions.question: Why was Drake's knighting highly significant for Anglo-Spanish relations?
questions.answer: It was a massive public insult to King Philip II. It proved to Spain that the English government officially supported the theft of Spanish gold.
questions.fact: Following the knighting, the furious Spanish ambassador, Bernardino de Mendoza, demanded Drake be handed over and hanged, but Elizabeth simply ignored him.
distractors: It forced Philip II to declare an immediate trade embargo on all English merchants. The Spanish King viewed the knighting as a direct insult.
It led to the Pope issuing the excommunication bull against Elizabeth. The Pope viewed the knighting as an endorsement of piracy.
It convinced France to sign a defensive alliance with England against Spain. The French viewed the knighting as a show of naval strength.

questions.question: Who legally owned the Netherlands during Elizabeth's reign?
questions.answer: Spain (King Philip II). The Netherlands was an incredibly wealthy province of the Spanish Empire.
questions.fact: The Netherlands was not a single country, but a collection of 17 distinct provinces, each fiercely protective of their own local laws and traditions against Spanish interference.
distractors: France (King Henry III). The Netherlands was an incredibly wealthy province of the French Empire.
England (Queen Elizabeth I). The Netherlands was an incredibly wealthy province of the English Empire.
The Holy Roman Empire (Emperor Rudolph II). The Netherlands was an incredibly wealthy province of the Empire.

questions.question: Why did the Dutch initially revolt against Spanish rule in 1566?
questions.answer: They rebelled against the brutal Catholic Inquisition and high Spanish taxes. The Protestant Dutch demanded religious and political freedom.
questions.fact: A major trigger for the revolt was Philip II's reorganisation of the Dutch bishoprics, which the locals saw as a direct attempt to force the Spanish Inquisition into their daily lives.
distractors: They rebelled against the English trade monopoly and high customs duties. The Protestant Dutch demanded economic and religious freedom.
They rebelled against the French military presence and high land rents. The Protestant Dutch demanded economic and religious freedom.
They rebelled against the German Protestant clergy and high church tithes. The Protestant Dutch demanded economic and religious freedom.

questions.question: Which Dutch Protestant rebel leader was assassinated in 1584, forcing Elizabeth to act?
questions.answer: William of Orange. His murder left the Dutch rebellion on the verge of collapse, forcing Elizabeth to intervene to stop a total Spanish victory.
questions.fact: William of Orange was killed by a Catholic assassin using a pistol - believed by historians to be the first time a head of state was ever assassinated with a handgun.
distractors: Maurice of Nassau. His murder left the Dutch rebellion on the verge of collapse, forcing Elizabeth to intervene to stop a total Spanish victory.
Johan van Oldenbarnevelt. His murder left the Dutch rebellion on the verge of collapse, forcing Elizabeth to intervene to stop a total Spanish victory.
Philip William of Orange. His murder left the Dutch rebellion on the verge of collapse, forcing Elizabeth to intervene to stop a total Spanish victory.

questions.question: What treaty did Elizabeth sign in 1585 to officially help the Dutch rebels?
questions.answer: The Treaty of Nonsuch. This effectively declared war on Spain by officially committing English money and soldiers to fight the Spanish army.
questions.fact: By signing this treaty, Elizabeth actually turned down the Dutch offer of becoming Queen of the Netherlands, as she still respected the idea that Philip II was the rightful sovereign.
distractors: The Treaty of Nonsuch. This effectively declared war on Spain by officially committing English money and soldiers to fight the Spanish army.
The Treaty of Nonsuch. This effectively declared war on Spain by officially committing English money and soldiers to fight the Spanish army.
The Treaty of Nonsuch. This effectively declared war on Spain by officially committing English money and soldiers to fight the Spanish army.

questions.question: How many English troops did Elizabeth promise to send to the Netherlands?
questions.answer: 7,400 troops. She agreed to finance this army to ensure the Dutch Protestants could hold out against the Duke of Parma.
questions.fact: Elizabeth promise to pay for the army, but she demanded the Dutch hand over two strategic ports (Flushing and Brill) as 'cautionary towns' to guarantee the English wouldn't be betrayed.
distractors: 3,000 troops. She agreed to finance this army to ensure the Dutch Protestants could hold out against the Duke of Parma.
5,000 troops. She agreed to finance this army to ensure the Dutch Protestants could hold out against the Duke of Parma.
10,000 troops. She agreed to finance this army to ensure the Dutch Protestants could hold out against the Duke of Parma.

questions.question: Who commanded the English army in the Netherlands?
questions.answer: Robert Dudley, Earl of Leicester. He was Elizabeth's favourite courtier but lacked the military genius to defeat the Spanish.
questions.fact: Dudley took an entire company of actors and musicians with him to the Netherlands, angering the Queen who felt he was treating a brutal warzone like a royal holiday.
distractors: Robert Devereux, Earl of Essex. He was Elizabeth's favourite courtier but lacked the military genius to defeat the Spanish.
Walter Raleigh, Earl of Devon. He was Elizabeth's favourite courtier but lacked the military genius to defeat the Spanish.
Thomas Sackville, Earl of Dorset. He was Elizabeth's favourite courtier but lacked the military genius to defeat the Spanish.

questions.question: What title did Dudley accept from the Dutch rebels that infuriated Elizabeth?
questions.answer: Governor-General. He accepted this title in 1586 without the Queen's permission.
questions.fact: Dudley claimed he only accepted the title because the desperate Dutch rebels begged him, but Elizabeth believed he was feeding his own massive personal ambition.
distractors: Lord Protector. He accepted this title in 1586 without the Queen's permission.
Stadtholder. He accepted this title in 1586 without the Queen's permission.
High Commissioner. He accepted this title in 1586 without the Queen's permission.

questions.question: Why did the title 'Governor-General' anger Elizabeth so much?
questions.answer: It implied England was trying to take over Spanish territory and depose Philip II. Elizabeth only wanted to protect the rebels, not conquer the Netherlands.
questions.fact: Elizabeth was so furious about the 'Governor-General' title that she sent a public letter to Dudley, forcing him to read out her severe reprimand in front of the Dutch leaders.
distractors: It implied England was trying to trade with Spanish colonies in the New World. Elizabeth only wanted to protect the rebels, not trade with the Americas.
It implied England was trying to excommunicate King Philip II. Elizabeth only wanted to protect the rebels, not challenge the Pope's authority.
It implied England was trying to force the Dutch to become Anglicans. Elizabeth only wanted to protect the rebels, not change their religion.

questions.question: Was Dudley's military campaign generally a success or a failure?
questions.answer: A failure. The campaign was severely underfunded, the troops were poorly supplied, and Elizabeth constantly held Dudley back from engaging in major battles.
questions.fact: Dudley's campaign was undermined by absolute treachery; two English Catholic officers, William Stanley and Rowland York, actually defected and handed their Dutch forts over to the Spanish!
distractors: A success. The campaign was highly funded, the troops were well supplied, and Elizabeth constantly encouraged Dudley to engage in major battles.
A partial success. The campaign managed to capture several major ports, but the troops suffered from lack of food and payment.
A stalemate. The campaign kept the Spanish busy but failed to capture any territory or prevent Spanish raids.

questions.question: Which vital deep-water port did Dudley successfully prevent the Spanish from capturing?
questions.answer: Ostend. Holding this port was critical because it meant the Spanish Armada later had no safe, deep harbor to dock in to load troops.
questions.fact: Because Dudley successfully secured Ostend, the Spanish Armada later had no choice but to drop anchor in the highly exposed, open waters off Calais, leaving them totally vulnerable to fireships.
distractors: Antwerp. Holding this port was critical because it meant the Spanish Armada later had no safe, deep harbor to dock in to load troops.
Flushing. Holding this port was critical because it meant the Spanish Armada later had no safe, deep harbor to dock in to load troops.
Brill. Holding this port was critical because it meant the Spanish Armada later had no safe, deep harbor to dock in to load troops.

questions.question: In what month and year did Drake attack Cadiz?
questions.answer: April 1587. Elizabeth sent him to disrupt the Spanish fleet as they were actively building the Armada.
questions.fact: Drake was given strict royal orders not to attack the Spanish coast, but he conveniently 'did not receive' the message until he had already set sail!
distractors: August 1585. Elizabeth sent him to disrupt the Spanish fleet as they were actively building the Armada.
October 1586. Elizabeth sent him to disrupt the Spanish fleet as they were actively building the Armada.
June 1588. Elizabeth sent him to disrupt the Spanish fleet as they were actively building the Armada.

questions.question: What was Cadiz?
questions.answer: A heavily fortified Spanish naval port. It was the main gathering point for Philip II's invasion fleet.
questions.fact: Cadiz was highly protected by shore batteries and oar-powered galleys, making Drake's decision to sail straight into the harbour an incredibly bold and reckless military manoeuvre.
distractors: A heavily fortified Spanish colony in Flanders. It was the main gathering point for Philip II's invasion fleet.
A heavily fortified Spanish military academy in Madrid. It was the main gathering point for Philip II's invasion fleet.
A heavily fortified Spanish trading post in the Caribbean. It was the main gathering point for Philip II's invasion fleet.

questions.question: What famous nickname was given to Drake's raid on Cadiz?
questions.answer: 'Singeing the King of Spain's beard'. This reflected how Drake humiliated the most powerful king in the world right on his own doorstep.
questions.fact: The phrase was a 16th-century joke implying that Philip II had been personally embarrassed and his pride burned, though his 'beard' (his naval strength) would eventually grow back.
distractors: Slapping the King of Spain's face. This reflected how Drake humiliated the most powerful king in the world right on his own doorstep.
Stealing the King of Spain's crown. This reflected how Drake humiliated the most powerful king in the world right on his own doorstep.
Burning the King of Spain's fleet. This reflected how Drake humiliated the most powerful king in the world right on his own doorstep.

questions.question: Roughly how many Spanish ships did Drake destroy in the harbour?
questions.answer: Around 30 ships. He launched a daring surprise attack, burning and sinking the vessels before the Spanish could respond.
questions.fact: Many of the ships Drake burned were large, slow-moving merchant vessels that were essential for carrying the heavy cannons and supplies the Armada needed to transport an army.
distractors: Around 10 ships. He launched a daring surprise attack, burning and sinking the vessels before the Spanish could respond.
Around 50 ships. He launched a daring surprise attack, burning and sinking the vessels before the Spanish could respond.
Around 100 ships. He launched a daring surprise attack, burning and sinking the vessels before the Spanish could respond.

questions.question: What vital wooden items did Drake destroy thousands of during the raid?
questions.answer: Barrel staves (seasoned wooden planks). These were essential for building watertight barrels.
questions.fact: Drake burned over 1.6 million individual barrel staves, causing a catastrophic logistical nightmare for the Spanish supply chain.
distractors: Oars. These were essential for powering the slow, heavy Spanish troop ships.
Planks. These were essential for repairing damaged hulls of the Spanish Armada.
Masts. These were essential for supporting sails on the Spanish galleons.

questions.question: Why were the barrel staves so important to the Spanish Armada?
questions.answer: They were needed to store food and water safely for the long journey to England. Without them, supplies could not be preserved.
questions.fact: In 16th-century naval warfare, sailors lived entirely on a diet of salted beef, hardtack biscuits, and weak beer - all of which absolutely required airtight barrels to survive the sea.
distractors: They were needed to construct the defensive barriers on the decks of the ships. Without them, soldiers were exposed to enemy fire.
They were needed to build the small transport barges to load troops. Without them, Medina Sidonia could not reach the shore.
They were needed to build secure storage boxes for the gunpowder. Without them, ammunition could easily get wet and useless.

questions.question: What happened to the Spanish food during the Armada because of the destroyed staves?
questions.answer: It had to be put in inferior, leaky barrels made of unseasoned wood, causing the food and water to rot quickly.
questions.fact: Because of the green, unseasoned wood used to replace the destroyed barrels, the Spanish water turned foul and the food rotted before the Armada even reached the English Channel.
distractors: It had to be left behind in Spain, causing the soldiers to go hungry during the entire voyage.
It was seized by English privateers in the Channel, causing the soldiers to go hungry during the retreat.
It had to be thrown overboard during the storms, causing the soldiers to go hungry during the retreat.

questions.question: After leaving Cadiz, what did Drake do off the coast of Portugal?
questions.answer: He continued to attack Spanish supply ships and captured the San Felipe, a ship loaded with gold and spices.
questions.fact: The San Felipe was a massive Portuguese carrack loaded with spices from the East Indies; the cargo was so incredibly valuable it paid for the entire Cadiz expedition single-handedly.
distractors: He blockaded the port of Lisbon to prevent the Armada from sailing, but was forced to retreat when Spanish reinforcements arrived.
He landed troops to launch a ground attack on Madrid, but was forced to retreat when Spanish reinforcements arrived.
He signed a secret trade agreement with Portuguese merchants, but was forced to cancel it when the Spanish King discovered the plot.

questions.question: How long did Drake's raid on Cadiz delay the launch of the Spanish Armada?
questions.answer: By a full year. The destruction of ships and supplies forced Philip II to halt the invasion until 1588.
questions.fact: The delay meant that Spain's finest admiral, the Marquis of Santa Cruz, died of old age before the Armada could launch, forcing Philip to appoint the inexperienced Medina Sidonia.
distractors: By a few weeks. The destruction of ships and supplies forced Philip II to halt the invasion until 1588.
By six months. The destruction of ships and supplies forced Philip II to halt the invasion until 1588.
By two years. The destruction of ships and supplies forced Philip II to halt the invasion until 1588.

questions.question: How long did this delay buy for England?
questions.answer: It bought Elizabeth crucial time to build up her own navy, train her militias, and prepare her coastal defences.
questions.fact: Elizabeth used this precious year to establish a massive network of warning beacons across the south coast of England, ready to be lit the moment the Spanish ships were spotted.
distractors: It bought Elizabeth crucial time to sign a defensive alliance with France and the Netherlands.
It bought Elizabeth crucial time to raise land rents and collect taxes to fund her army.
It bought Elizabeth crucial time to negotiate a peace treaty with the Pope and Spain.

questions.question: In what year did the Spanish Armada finally launch?
questions.answer: 1588. The massive fleet set sail from Lisbon, Portugal, towards the English Channel.
questions.fact: The Armada actually launched in May 1588, but encountered terrible storms immediately and had to turn back to port for repairs, not reaching England until late July.
distractors: 1585. The massive fleet set sail from Lisbon, Portugal, towards the English Channel.
1586. The massive fleet set sail from Lisbon, Portugal, towards the English Channel.
1587. The massive fleet set sail from Lisbon, Portugal, towards the English Channel.

questions.question: How many ships made up the Spanish Armada?
questions.answer: 130 ships. It was the largest invasion fleet Europe had ever seen, carrying roughly 30,000 sailors and soldiers.
questions.fact: Despite being huge, less than half of the 130 ships were actual purpose-built warships; many were hastily converted merchant vessels or slow-moving transport hulks.
distractors: 50 ships. It was the largest invasion fleet Europe had ever seen, carrying roughly 30,000 sailors and soldiers.
100 ships. It was the largest invasion fleet Europe had ever seen, carrying roughly 30,000 sailors and soldiers.
200 ships. It was the largest invasion fleet Europe had ever seen, carrying roughly 30,000 sailors and soldiers.

questions.question: Who was chosen by Philip II to command the Armada?
questions.answer: The Duke of Medina Sidonia. He was an incredibly wealthy noble chosen for his rank, rather than his military skill.
questions.fact: Medina Sidonia was actually an excellent administrator and organiser, successfully gathering massive amounts of weapons and food, but he completely lacked tactical combat experience.
distractors: The Duke of Parma. He was an incredibly wealthy noble chosen for his rank, rather than his military skill.
The Duke of Guise. He was an incredibly wealthy noble chosen for his rank, rather than his military skill.
Don John of Austria. He was an incredibly wealthy noble chosen for his rank, rather than his military skill.

questions.question: Why was the Duke of Medina Sidonia a poor choice for a naval commander?
questions.answer: He had absolutely no maritime experience and suffered terribly from seasickness. He actually begged the King not to send him.
questions.fact: When appointed, Medina Sidonia wrote a desperate letter to Philip II stating he knew nothing of the sea and caught a cold whenever he went on a boat!
distractors: He was too old and could not physically withstand the strain of a long voyage. He actually begged the King not to send him.
He was a Protestant sympathizer who secretly wanted England to win the war. He actually begged the King not to send him.
He was a soldier who preferred land warfare and refused to board the ship. He actually begged the King not to send him.

questions.question: What was the Armada's primary strategic plan?
questions.answer: To sail to the Netherlands, pick up the Duke of Parma's 27,000 troops, transport them across the Channel, and march on London.
questions.fact: The Spanish plan heavily relied on English Catholics rising up to join Parma's army once they landed in Kent, but this domestic uprising never happened.
distractors: To launch a surprise attack on London from the Thames. This would allow the Spanish to capture the Queen and declare victory.
To blockade the English Channel and starve the country into surrender. This would allow the Spanish to dictate terms of peace.
To land troops in Ireland and launch a joint invasion with Irish Catholics. This would allow the Spanish to march on London from the West.

questions.question: What defensive shape did the Spanish fleet sail in as they travelled up the English Channel?
questions.answer: A tightly packed crescent formation. This protected the slower, vulnerable supply ships in the centre from English cannon fire.
questions.fact: The crescent formation was so tightly disciplined that the English navy chased the Armada up the Channel for a full week without managing to sink a single Spanish ship.
distractors: A straight line formation. This allowed the faster galleons to lead the attack and fire their cannons at the English fleet.
A circle formation. This allowed the ships to defend themselves from all sides against English privateer raids.
A wedge formation. This allowed the heavy transports to smash through the English defensive lines in the Channel.

questions.question: Where did the Spanish Armada drop anchor on 6 August 1588?
questions.answer: Calais. They stopped here to wait for messages from the Duke of Parma.
questions.fact: Medina Sidonia dropped anchor at Calais because he discovered the Duke of Parma's troops were delayed by disease and Dutch blockades, and were not ready to board the fleet.
distractors: Dover. They stopped here to wait for messages from the Duke of Parma.
Gravelines. They stopped here to wait for messages from the Duke of Parma.
Ostend. They stopped here to wait for messages from the Duke of Parma.

questions.question: What tactic did the English use to break the Spanish crescent formation at Calais?
questions.answer: They sent eight burning fireships into the anchored fleet in the middle of the night. This caused mass panic, forcing the Spanish to cut their anchors and scatter.
questions.fact: The Spanish captains were terrified of the fireships because they thought they were 'Hellburners' - special floating bombs packed with gunpowder and shrapnel previously used by the Dutch.
distractors: They used long-range cannons to sink the slower supply ships. This forced the Spanish to scatter and retreat.
They launched a pre-emptive board attack on the flagship San Martin. This forced the Spanish to scatter and retreat.
They simulated a retreat to draw the Spanish galleons away. This forced the Spanish to scatter and retreat.

questions.question: What decisive naval battle took place on 8 August 1588?
questions.answer: The Battle of Gravelines. The English attacked the scattered Spanish ships, dealing heavy damage and preventing them from linking up with Parma.
questions.fact: During the brutal Battle of Gravelines, the English cannons fired so rapidly that they completely ran out of heavy ammunition, forcing them to back away from the Spanish.
distractors: The Battle of Cadiz. The English attacked the scattered Spanish ships, dealing heavy damage and preventing them from linking up with Parma.
The Battle of Calais. The English attacked the scattered Spanish ships, dealing heavy damage and preventing them from linking up with Parma.
The Battle of Dover. The English attacked the scattered Spanish ships, dealing heavy damage and preventing them from linking up with Parma.

questions.question: What route was the Armada forced to take to retreat back to Spain?
questions.answer: North, around the treacherous coasts of Scotland and Ireland. The wind direction and the English fleet blocked their return down the Channel.
questions.fact: Because they lacked accurate charts of the Scottish and Irish coastlines, Spanish captains literally had to guess their latitude by observing the sun in freezing, cloudy conditions.
distractors: South, directly through the English Channel under the cover of night. The wind direction and the English fleet blocked their return.
West, across the open Atlantic towards the Azores. The wind direction and the English fleet blocked their return.
East, through the Baltic Sea and around Scandinavia. The wind direction and the English fleet blocked their return.

questions.question: What type of galleons did the English use that were faster and more maneuverable?
questions.answer: 'Race-built' galleons. These had lower castles and sleeker hulls, making them much faster than the bulky Spanish troop transports.
questions.fact: These sleeker ships were heavily championed by John Hawkins, who lowered the tall 'castles' at the front and back of the ships to make them less top-heavy and much faster.
distractors: Caravels. These had lower castles and sleeker hulls, making them much faster than the bulky Spanish troop transports.
Carracks. These had lower castles and sleeker hulls, making them much faster than the bulky Spanish troop transports.
Galleasses. These had lower castles and sleeker hulls, making them much faster than the bulky Spanish troop transports.

questions.question: What advantage did English cannons (culverins) have over Spanish cannons?
questions.answer: They were mounted on smaller gun carriages, allowing them to be pulled back, reloaded, and fired much faster than Spanish guns.
questions.fact: Spanish cannons were incredibly difficult to reload at sea; many were fired once and then abandoned, whereas English gun crews were trained to reload and fire multiple times an hour.
distractors: They had a longer range and could fire heavier iron balls. This allowed the English to batter the Spanish ships from safety.
They were mounted on swivel stands and could be aimed in any direction. This allowed the English to fire at any angle.
They used advanced gunpowder that did not create smoke. This allowed the English to see the enemy clearly during battle.

questions.question: What tactic did the English use at Gravelines to avoid Spanish strengths?
questions.answer: They kept their distance and battered the ships with long-range cannons, completely avoiding the close-quarters boarding tactics the Spanish infantry relied upon.
questions.fact: The Spanish heavily relied on grappling hooks to pull enemy ships close so their elite infantry could board; English captains strictly ordered their men to stay out of grappling range.
distractors: They launched fireships into the scattered fleet during the day, preventing Medina Sidonia from reloading his guns.
They blockaded the harbor to trap the Spanish ships inside, preventing them from engaging in naval maneuvers.
They used speed to launch quick hit-and-run attacks on the slower transports, avoiding the heavy galleons completely.

questions.question: How did poor communication ruin the Spanish plan?
questions.answer: It took a week for messages to reach the Duke of Parma by sea, meaning his troops were not ready to board the Armada when it arrived.
questions.fact: By the time Medina Sidonia's slow messengers finally reached the Duke of Parma by land, the Armada had already been scattered by the fireships at Calais!
distractors: It took a week for messages to reach the King in Spain, meaning Medina Sidonia did not have permission to attack the English fleet.
It took a week for messages to reach the French allies, meaning the Duke of Guise did not bring his fleet to assist.
It took a week for messages to reach the Pope in Rome, meaning the Armada did not receive the holy blessing before the battle.

questions.question: Why couldn't the Duke of Parma's troops safely board the Armada in the Netherlands?
questions.answer: Because Spain lacked a deep-water port (like Ostend), meaning Parma's troops had to be loaded onto small barges, making them sitting ducks for attack.
questions.fact: Parma's troops were waiting in flat-bottomed barges; if they tried to sail out to meet the Armada, they would have been instantly slaughtered by shallow-water Dutch rebel ships.
distractors: Because the English navy had blockaded the Dutch ports, preventing the barges from leaving the canals.
Because the Dutch rebels had launched a land attack on Parma's camp, forcing him to defend his base.
Because the Spanish soldiers refused to board the small barges, demanding larger warships for safety.

questions.question: How did Drake's 1587 raid contribute to the Spanish defeat in 1588?
questions.answer: The rotting food and contaminated water (due to the leaky barrels) caused widespread illness, starvation, and low morale among the Spanish sailors.
questions.fact: The dysentery and food poisoning caused by the rotting supplies from the Cadiz raid killed far more Spanish sailors than actual English cannon fire during the battles.
distractors: The destruction of the timber forests in Spain meant Medina Sidonia had to build ships with inferior wood, causing them to leak.
The destruction of the gold shipment meant Spain could not pay the soldiers, causing a mutiny before the launch.
The destruction of the naval academy meant Spain had no trained captains, causing confusion during the naval maneuvers.

questions.question: What role did the weather play in the total destruction of the Armada?
questions.answer: Severe gale-force winds blew the retreating Spanish ships onto rocks off the coasts of Scotland and Ireland, causing mass shipwrecks.
questions.fact: When starving Spanish sailors managed to swim ashore after wrecking on the Irish coast, they were often ruthlessly hunted down and executed by English troops stationed in Ireland.
distractors: Extreme heat caused the gunpowder to explode in the hot holds of the Spanish galleons, sinking several ships.
Heavy fog in the Channel caused the Spanish ships to collide with each other in the dark, sinking several ships.
Lack of wind in the Channel left the Spanish ships sitting ducks for English cannon fire, sinking several ships.

questions.question: What term was used by English propaganda to describe these destructive storms?
questions.answer: The 'Protestant Wind'. The English claimed that God had sent the storms to deliberately protect Protestantism and destroy the Catholics.
questions.fact: To celebrate the victory, Elizabeth minted a special commemorative medal with the Latin inscription: 'He blew with His winds, and they were scattered.'
distractors: The 'Wind of God'. The English claimed that God had sent the storms to deliberately protect Protestantism and destroy the Catholics.
The 'Divine Breeze'. The English claimed that God had sent the storms to deliberately protect Protestantism and destroy the Catholics.
The 'Anglican Storm'. The English claimed that God had sent the storms to deliberately protect Protestantism and destroy the Catholics.

questions.question: Out of the original 130, how many Spanish ships managed to survive and return to Spain?
questions.answer: Only 65 ships. Thousands of Spanish sailors died of disease, starvation, or drowned during the retreat.
questions.fact: The men who did return to Spain were kept in quarantine on their ships for days because they were ridden with typhus and disease, causing many more to die in the harbour.
distractors: Only 30 ships. Thousands of Spanish sailors died of disease, starvation, or drowned during the retreat.
Only 50 ships. Thousands of Spanish sailors died of disease, starvation, or drowned during the retreat.
Only 100 ships. Thousands of Spanish sailors died of disease, starvation, or drowned during the retreat.

questions.question: How did the victory impact Elizabeth's image?
questions.answer: It gave her a massive propaganda boost, celebrated in the 'Armada Portrait', proving to the world that England was a dominant naval power.
questions.fact: The famous 'Armada Portrait' features Elizabeth resting her hand firmly on a globe, projecting powerful Tudor propaganda that England was now the dominant master of the oceans.
distractors: It convinced Parliament to name her as the absolute ruler of England, giving her the power to govern without consulting them.
It convinced the Pope to lift the excommunication bull and recognize her as the rightful Queen of England.
It convinced France to sign a trade treaty that lowered taxes on English cloth, boosting the economy.

questions.question: Name the philosophical movement that heavily influenced the expansion of Elizabethan education.
questions.answer: Humanism. Humanists believed that education was essential to fulfil human potential and create a civilised society, not just for training priests.
questions.fact: Humanists like Desiderius Erasmus greatly influenced Elizabeth herself; he was a close friend of her famous childhood tutor, Roger Ascham.
distractors: Scholasticism. Humanists believed that education was essential to fulfil human potential and create a civilised society, not just for training priests.
Rationalism. Humanists believed that education was essential to fulfil human potential and create a civilised society, not just for training priests.
Empiricism. Humanists believed that education was essential to fulfil human potential and create a civilised society, not just for training priests.

questions.question: What type of school was founded for the sons of the 'middling sort' (gentry and wealthy merchants)?
questions.answer: Grammar schools. During Elizabeth's reign, 72 new fee-paying grammar schools were opened across the country.
questions.fact: A typical school day in an Elizabethan grammar school was brutal; it lasted from 6:00 AM to 5:00 PM, with only a short break for lunch!
distractors: Petty schools. During Elizabeth's reign, 72 new fee-paying grammar schools were opened across the country.
Dame schools. During Elizabeth's reign, 72 new fee-paying grammar schools were opened across the country.
Public schools. During Elizabeth's reign, 72 new fee-paying grammar schools were opened across the country.

questions.question: What two classical subjects were heavily focused on in grammar schools?
questions.answer: Latin and Greek. These were considered the language of the educated elite and were essential for anyone wanting to study law or medicine.
questions.fact: Speaking English was often strictly forbidden in grammar schools; boys caught speaking it instead of Latin could be physically beaten by the schoolmaster.
distractors: Mathematics and geometry. These were considered the language of the educated elite and were essential for anyone wanting to study law or medicine.
History and geography. These were considered the language of the educated elite and were essential for anyone wanting to study law or medicine.
Theology and scripture. These were considered the language of the educated elite and were essential for anyone wanting to study law or medicine.

questions.question: What were 'Petty Schools'?
questions.answer: Small, local schools that taught basic reading, writing, and arithmetic to younger boys before they went to grammar school or an apprenticeship.
questions.fact: Petty schools often used a 'hornbook' - a piece of wood with the alphabet and the Lord's Prayer written on it, protected by a thin, transparent layer of animal horn.
distractors: Fee-paying schools run by the clergy. They taught advanced Latin, Greek, and theology to prepare boys for university.
Home schools run by the gentry. They taught social skills, languages, and fencing to prepare noble children for court.
Free schools run by local town guilds. They taught trade skills and merchant accounting to prepare boys for apprenticeships.

questions.question: What were 'Dame Schools'?
questions.answer: Very basic schools run by local women in their homes, usually providing a minimal education for girls.
questions.fact: Women running Dame Schools often taught the children basic reading while simultaneously doing their own household chores like spinning wool or baking bread.
distractors: Advanced boarding schools for noble girls. They taught foreign languages, music, and embroidery to prepare them for marriage.
Catholic schools run by nuns in secret. They taught Latin liturgy and catechism to Catholic children in the North.
Special schools for training female servants. They taught cooking, cleaning, and sewing to prepare girls for work.

questions.question: How were the children of the wealthy nobility educated?
questions.answer: By private tutors in their own homes. They learned foreign languages, history, philosophy, and social skills like fencing (for boys) or needlework (for girls).
questions.fact: Noble girls were highly educated in music, singing, and dancing because these were considered essential social skills to attract a wealthy, powerful husband at court.
distractors: By attending prestigious grammar schools in London, such as St. Paul's. They lived in boarding houses and studied classical subjects.
By attending universities at Oxford and Cambridge from a very young age. They lived in college quarters and studied law.
By attending parish schools run by the local Protestant bishop. They studied theology and bible readings alongside local children.

questions.question: Name the only two universities in Elizabethan England.
questions.answer: Oxford and Cambridge. They expanded during this period to train the clergy and professional classes in subjects like geometry, astronomy, and rhetoric.
questions.fact: Students at university were often very young; it was highly common for boys to start their university degrees at just 14 or 15 years old.
distractors: London and Oxford. They expanded during this period to train the clergy and professional classes in subjects like geometry, astronomy, and rhetoric.
Cambridge and London. They expanded during this period to train the clergy and professional classes in subjects like geometry, astronomy, and rhetoric.
Oxford and Bristol. They expanded during this period to train the clergy and professional classes in subjects like geometry, astronomy, and rhetoric.

questions.question: What was the approximate male literacy rate by the end of Elizabeth's reign?
questions.answer: Around 30%. This significant improvement was driven by the new grammar schools and the practical need for merchants to read contracts.
questions.fact: The ability to read out loud was much higher than the ability to write, as writing required expensive paper, ink, and a completely separate skill set.
distractors: Around 10%. This significant improvement was driven by the new grammar schools and the practical need for merchants to read contracts.
Around 50%. This significant improvement was driven by the new grammar schools and the practical need for merchants to read contracts.
Around 70%. This significant improvement was driven by the new grammar schools and the practical need for merchants to read contracts.

questions.question: What was the approximate female literacy rate by the end of the period?
questions.answer: Around 10%. Female literacy remained very low because society believed girls did not need a formal education and should focus on domestic skills.
questions.fact: Even though female literacy was low overall, London had a much higher rate of female literacy compared to the rural countryside due to the concentration of merchant families.
distractors: Around 1%. Female literacy remained very low because society believed girls did not need a formal education and should focus on domestic skills.
Around 5%. Female literacy remained very low because society believed girls did not need a formal education and should focus on domestic skills.
Around 20%. Female literacy remained very low because society believed girls did not need a formal education and should focus on domestic skills.

questions.question: How did the Protestant religion encourage the growth of education?
questions.answer: Protestants believed everyone should be able to read the Bible in English for themselves, motivating parishes to teach basic literacy.
questions.fact: The government also ordered that a copy of John Foxe's 'Book of Martyrs' - a gruesome book detailing the deaths of Protestants under Mary I - be placed in cathedrals alongside the Bible.
distractors: Protestants believed that the Church should run all grammar schools, motivating parishes to raise funds for new school buildings.
Protestants believed that only educated men could understand the sermons, motivating parishes to teach advanced Latin.
Protestants believed that learning classical subjects would help fight Catholic arguments, motivating parishes to teach philosophy.

questions.question: Name a sport played almost exclusively by the wealthy nobility.
questions.answer: Real tennis (or hawking/fencing). These sports required expensive equipment, horses, or purpose-built indoor courts that only the rich could afford.
questions.fact: Elizabeth's father, Henry VIII, was a massive fan of Real Tennis and built a famous indoor court at Hampton Court Palace, which is still used to this day!
distractors: Mob football. These sports required expensive equipment, horses, or purpose-built indoor courts that only the rich could afford.
Bear-baiting. These sports required expensive equipment, horses, or purpose-built indoor courts that only the rich could afford.
Archery. These sports required expensive equipment, horses, or purpose-built indoor courts that only the rich could afford.

questions.question: What was 'mob football'?
questions.answer: A highly violent, rule-less game played by the lower classes. It often involved hundreds of men from rival villages fighting to push a ball into the opponent's area.
questions.fact: Mob football was so violent, destructive to property, and occasionally resulted in deaths that the government repeatedly tried (and failed) to ban it.
distractors: A game similar to tennis played on grass courts by the gentry. It was a rule-based game that focused on physical agility and skill.
A game similar to cricket played in public parks by the merchant class. It was a rule-based game that focused on batting and fielding.
A game similar to rugby played in schoolyards by grammar school boys. It was a rule-based game that focused on running and passing.

questions.question: Name two popular 'cruel sports' watched by all social classes.
questions.answer: Bear-baiting and cock-fighting. People from all backgrounds would mix to watch these violent animal fights and bet large sums of money on the outcome.
questions.fact: Queen Elizabeth herself loved bear-baiting so much that she once personally overruled Parliament to prevent them from banning the bloody sport on Sundays.
distractors: Mob football and bear-baiting. People from all backgrounds would mix to watch these violent animal fights and bet large sums of money on the outcome.
Archery and cock-fighting. People from all backgrounds would mix to watch these violent animal fights and bet large sums of money on the outcome.
Real tennis and bear-baiting. People from all backgrounds would mix to watch these violent animal fights and bet large sums of money on the outcome.

questions.question: What was a 'bear garden'?
questions.answer: A purpose-built arena in London designed specifically for crowds to watch bear-baiting.
questions.fact: The 'Paris Garden' in Southwark was the most famous bear-baiting arena in London, located right next to the new theatres like the Globe.
distractors: A royal park in London where the Queen kept her private collection of bears. It was used for royal guests to walk and view the animals.
A local marketplace where trained bears performed tricks and danced for crowds. It was a popular form of street entertainment.
A specialized cage in the zoo where bears were bred for export to European courts. It was a profitable trade business in London.

questions.question: What change occurred in the type of plays being performed during Elizabeth's reign?
questions.answer: Traditional Catholic 'mystery plays' were replaced by secular (non-religious) plays, such as comedies and tragedies.
questions.fact: The government actively encouraged secular plays because they worried that the traditional Catholic 'mystery plays' would spark religious riots among the audience.
distractors: Traditional secular comedies were replaced by religious mystery plays detailing stories from the Bible to educate the public.
Traditional court performances in Latin were replaced by popular street theater in English to entertain the lower classes.
Traditional musical performances were replaced by complex poetry recitals written by court poets to show off their humanist education.

questions.question: Name one of the first purpose-built theatres constructed outside London's city walls.
questions.answer: The Theatre, The Curtain, The Rose, or The Globe.
questions.fact: Theatres were built outside the city walls in areas like Southwark because the Puritan-run City of London Corporation heavily restricted them inside the city limits.
distractors: The Globe, The Rose, or The Fortune.
The Curtain, The Rose, or The Fortune.
The Swan, The Rose, or The Fortune.

questions.question: How much did the poor pay to stand in the 'pit' at the theatre?
questions.answer: One penny. These audience members, known as 'groundlings', made the theatre an incredibly popular and accessible form of mass entertainment.
questions.fact: Groundlings were notoriously rowdy; they often threw cheap food like apples and hazelnuts at the actors if they didn't like the performance!
distractors: Two pennies. These audience members, known as 'groundlings', made the theatre an incredibly popular and accessible form of mass entertainment.
Three pennies. These audience members, known as 'groundlings', made the theatre an incredibly popular and accessible form of mass entertainment.
A halfpenny. These audience members, known as 'groundlings', made the theatre an incredibly popular and accessible form of mass entertainment.

questions.question: Where did the wealthy sit in the theatre?
questions.answer: In the covered galleries. They paid more money for comfortable seating, protection from the weather, and to show off their high social status.
questions.fact: The absolute richest nobles could pay a premium to sit on a stool right on the stage itself, making themselves part of the spectacle to show off their fine clothes.
distractors: In the front rows of the pit. They paid more money for comfortable seating, protection from the weather, and to show off their high social status.
On the stage itself alongside the actors. They paid more money for comfortable seating, protection from the weather, and to show off their high social status.
In private boxes behind the stage. They paid more money for comfortable seating, protection from the weather, and to show off their high social status.

questions.question: Why did acting troupes need the patronage of wealthy nobles?
questions.answer: To avoid being arrested as vagabonds. By being sponsored by a noble (like Leicester’s Men or The Queen’s Men), actors were legally protected as servants.
questions.fact: 'Leicester's Men' (sponsored by Robert Dudley) was the first acting company to receive a royal patent in 1574, legally protecting them from being whipped under the Vagabonds Act.
distractors: To raise the massive funds needed to build theatres in London. Without the backing of a noble, they could not afford the building licenses.
To write the scripts and design the costumes for the performances. Without the backing of a noble, they lacked the education and style.
To avoid paying taxes on their ticket sales to the city officials. Without the backing of a noble, they were treated as illegal businesses.

questions.question: Which strict religious group heavily opposed the theatre?
questions.answer: The Puritans. They believed theatres were the work of the devil, encouraged sinful behaviour, and distracted people from prayer.
questions.fact: Puritans actively preached that the terrifying outbreaks of the bubonic plague were a direct punishment from God for the sin of attending the theatre.
distractors: Moderate Catholics. They believed theatres were the work of the devil, encouraged sinful behaviour, and distracted people from prayer.
High Church Anglicans. They believed theatres were the work of the devil, encouraged sinful behaviour, and distracted people from prayer.
Humanist scholars. They believed theatres were the work of the devil, encouraged sinful behaviour, and distracted people from prayer.

questions.question: What happened to the population of England during Elizabeth's reign?
questions.answer: It grew rapidly from 3 million in 1551 to 4.2 million by 1601. This demographic boom created massive pressure on food and jobs.
questions.fact: The population of London grew faster than any other European city, swelling from around 50,000 in 1500 to over 200,000 by 1600.
distractors: It grew slowly from 3 million in 1551 to 3.5 million by 1601. This demographical growth created moderate pressure on food and jobs.
It grew rapidly from 2 million in 1551 to 4 million by 1601. This demographic boom created massive pressure on food and jobs.
It grew rapidly from 4 million in 1551 to 6 million by 1601. This demographic boom created massive pressure on food and jobs.

questions.question: How did this rapid population growth affect wages?
questions.answer: It drove wages down. Because there was a massive surplus of available workers, competition for jobs was fierce, allowing employers to pay less.
questions.fact: Because of the massive surplus of workers, real wages for a labouring family dropped to their lowest point in over 300 years during Elizabeth's reign.
distractors: It drove wages up. Because there was a massive shortage of available workers, employers had to pay more to attract labor.
It had no effect on wages. Because wages were strictly regulated by the Justices of the Peace in each county.
It caused wages to fluctuate wildly. Because the cloth trade was constantly disrupted by the war with Spain.

questions.question: What agricultural practice involved fencing off traditional 'common land'?
questions.answer: Enclosure. This denied ordinary peasants their traditional right to graze their animals or forage for firewood, often resulting in eviction.
questions.fact: Enclosure sparked several minor riots in the countryside, as desperate peasants tore down the new wooden fences in the night to reclaim their traditional grazing rights.
distractors: Enclosure. This allowed peasants to buy their own fields and farm them efficiently, improving the agricultural economy.
Enclosure. This was a system of crop rotation that increased the harvest yield and reduced food prices in the country.
Enclosure. This was a method of draining marshes to create new fertile fields for growing wheat and barley.

questions.question: Why did many landowners switch from growing crops to farming sheep?
questions.answer: The international wool trade was booming. Woollen cloth accounted for 81.6% of England's exports, making sheep farming highly profitable.
questions.fact: English wool was considered some of the finest in the world, making it highly prized and incredibly expensive for weavers in Italy and the Netherlands.
distractors: The government offered financial grants for sheep farming. Landowners hoped to receive these subsidies to pay off their land debts.
Sheep farming was easier to manage than crop farming. Landowners wanted to reduce the number of workers on their estates for safety.
The demand for meat was growing rapidly in the towns. Landowners wanted to supply the butcher guilds for high profits.

questions.question: How did the shift to sheep farming directly cause rural unemployment?
questions.answer: Sheep farming required significantly less physical labour than crop farming. This meant thousands of farm labourers were suddenly fired and left homeless.
questions.fact: A single shepherd and his dog could watch over a flock of hundreds of sheep, instantly replacing the jobs of dozens of manual crop farmers.
distractors: Sheep farming required more physical labour than crop farming. This created a labor shortage that landowners could not afford to pay.
Sheep farming led to a decline in the fertility of the soil. This meant that landowners could not grow crops and had to lay off workers.
